rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the text: it proposes to 'pay for actual energy savings over time' via a 'utility incentive program' (policy_statement; verbatim_text). Energy savings in buildings implies reduced energy use and thus GHG mitigation. The gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token found') reflects tokenization/keyword mismatch rather than lack of mitigation content. Climate screen is 'explicit_self', which breaks the tie in favor of inclusion.
policy_statement: Pilot a utility incentive program that pays for actual energy savings over time rather than up-front payments for projected savings (by 2015).
mechanism_description: Pilot a utility incentive program that pays over time for actual energy savings, encouraging real efficiency improvements that reduce overall energy use.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service demand → ↓ delivered energy use → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Utilities and customers adopt a pay-for-performance incentive structure that rewards measured energy savings, driving implementation of efficiency upgrades (equipment, controls, envelope, commissioning) and improved operations in buildings.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Efficiency measures reduce energy set for the same level of heating/cooling/lighting and other services, decreasing electricity and/or on-site fuel consumption.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced upstream and on-site combustion leads to lower CO₂ (and other) emissions associated with building energy use.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from lower building energy demand (and therefore less fuel burned and/or less grid generation).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Incentives → verified efficiency actions → lower energy consumption → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While it can improve energy affordability (resource efficiency co-benefit), the main climate outcome is emissions mitigation via reduced energy demand and generation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the grid is already near-zero-carbon, emissions reductions may be smaller, but the mechanism still primarily targets mitigation through demand reduction; if savings primarily reduce peak demand, it still reduces marginal generation/emissions.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using “utility efficiency incentive program paying for actual savings” is fundamentally an energy-efficiency demand-reduction mechanism that reduces emissions intensity-weighted energy use.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (electric vs gas savings, retrofit vs operations, peak vs annual, equity targeting) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Emissions impact depends on the marginal emissions factor of avoided energy (time- and location-varying) and on additionality/free-ridership; in rare cases with fully decarbonized supply, benefits shift toward non-climate co-benefits, but the dominant intended pathway remains mitigation via energy-use reduction.
key_indicators: "Pilot a utility incentive program"; "pay for actual energy savings over time"; "instead of providing an up-front payment for projected savings"
co_benefits: Lower customer energy bills; improved building comfort/health; reduced local air pollutants
instance_edge_case_notes: “Pilot” could be interpreted as a planning/strategy step, but the described policy instrument is explicitly an incentive program design (pay-for-performance), so it is classified as an incentive-program.
